text: Psalm 81, this evening, if you have your Bible with you, Psalm 81, and for a number of weeks now, on Wednesday evening, we've been walking through this psalm. Of course, we started dealing with the fact that it is a psalm that was related to the Feast of Tabernacles and dealt with what that is and how that was important for the children of Israel to be mindful of God's deliverance and all that He did. And we've walked through the psalm in our last study. Of course, last week was prayer, praise, and pie, but prior to that, we looked at the end of verse number seven where the Lord said, I proved thee at the waters of Meribah, Sila. And we talked about that proving and how that God was challenging them, teaching them, proving them, and how that He'll prove you and I as well in our lives. As we move forward through the end of this chapter, just one psalm, of course, but it's interesting because in the final nine verses of Psalm 81, you will notice that the word hearken is used three times. And it's not always in the form of hearken. It's hearken or hearkened. But the reality is that three times in nine verses, we see that word. And these three references really provide a simple outline for the last nine verses of this particular psalm, and it will be for our next three lessons. And tonight, we'll deal with verses eight through 10. The title is this, wilt thou hearken? Wilt thou hearken? Next week on Wednesday evening, we'll look at the next couple of verses and deal with this. They hearkened not. Then as we finish up Psalm 81 and we finish the last of these verses in that thought, the title for that message will be, what if they hearkened? And think about that. In these nine verses, we see the Lord speaking to his people and he says it this way, if thou wilt hearken unto me in verse number eight, and I've titled it, wilt thou hearken? And then we find and we understand as we'd read the Old Testament, that God will say next, they hearkened not. Isn't that a sad verse of Scripture to consider that God was giving instruction, he was giving what was good and beneficial for his people, but they would not listen, they would not hearken. And then we'll finish up, what if they had hearkened? What if they did, what do we miss out when we don't obey what God tells us to do? And we'll look at this as we study throughout the remainder of this year. The word hearken means to attend, it means to regard, it means to give heed to what is uttered, to observe or to obey. The children of Israel are familiar with this word in Deuteronomy chapter four, you don't need to turn there, I'll just read it and come right back to Psalm 81. But as the Lord is preparing his people for entrance into the land, Moses says to them in Deuteronomy four and verse one, now therefore hearken, O Israel, under the statutes and under the judgments which I teach you for to do them, that ye may live and go in and possess the land which the Lord God of your fathers giveth you. And so as we look at the text of Scripture this evening in verses eight through 10, notice the Bible says, hear, O my people, and I will testify unto thee, O Israel, if thou wilt hearken unto me, there shall no strange God be in thee, neither shalt thou worship any strange God. I am the Lord thy God, which brought thee out of the land of Egypt, open thy mouth wide, and I will fill it. As we consider the thought this evening, wilt thou hearken, four simple thoughts for you from these few verses of Scripture. Number one, the Lord instructs. Aren't you thankful that God gives instruction? You know, we are a blessed and privileged group of people to have a Bible. It's a unique privilege that we have. We understand that here in the midst of verse number eight, notice the Lord says, hear, O my people, notice this, and I will testify unto thee, if, O Israel, if thou wilt hearken unto me. In that middle phrase, when he says, I will testify unto thee, the word testify means to make a solemn declaration, whether verbal or written, to establish some fact. It carries with it this idea, to give testimony for the purpose of communicating to others a knowledge of something not known to them. Now think about that just for a second with me, that God is saying, as he's speaking to his people, I will testify unto thee. I'm going to communicate something to you that is beneficial for you, something that is not known by you, and I'm going to give that to you. I'm gonna testify, I'm going to instruct. I think about the truth in Psalm 68, just a few pages back in your Bible, the Bible says this in verse number 11 of Psalm 68, the Lord gave the word, and it continues and says, great was the company of those that published it, but I want you to consider that first phrase, the Lord gave the word. I'm thankful for the instruction that God has given to us. I'm thankful that all scripture is given by inspiration of God, and we tonight, as we look at this passage of scripture to consider that thought, wilt thou hearken, that we find that God says to his people, hear, O my people, and I will testify unto thee. He was telling them, I'm going to give you something that you don't know that is beneficial for you, that is advantageous that you know this. Hold your spot here in the book of Psalms and turn over quickly to the book of Romans, if you will, as we consider the New Testament, we think about this truth in Romans chapter three, we consider that God in Psalm 81, obviously is speaking to his people, his children, the people of Israel, and we find that today, as we look at us as New Testament church and understanding that God has given to us the whole canon of scripture, but in Romans chapter number three, there's an interesting aspect in which we notice, we open up into this chapter, thinking about even what is said there in Psalm 81 with regard to the Lord testifying unto his people. Notice, if you will, in Romans three, in verse number one, the Bible says, what advantage then hath the Jew? Or what profit is there of circumcision? Now, you know, we've spent time on this in the past that throughout the New Testament, there's readily used the term circumcision to refer to Jews and the term uncircumcision to refer to Gentiles. And so here in verse number one, he's asking these questions twofold, what is the profit? What is the advantage of being a Jew? And then notice what the Bible says in verse number two, much every way, chiefly, notice, because that unto them were committed the oracles of God. You know, the children of Israel in Old Testament, obviously, God's dealing with his people, he committed to them, he gave them his word, and he's instructing them, he's teaching them, and he's giving to them, as we noted in Psalm 81 in verse number eight, he's providing, communicating to them something that is unknown to them, but will benefit them. And it's important that we understand this evening that we have the whole Bible, and God has given to us his word. And as we consider this evening, that the Lord instructs, and we even ask the question that is asked here in verse number eight, wilt thou hearken, if thou wilt hearken unto me? Will we hear, will we take heed to, will we give regard, will we desire and determine that we will observe or obey God's word as we hear it, understanding that God's word is instruction for us. Notice, not only do we see the Lord instructs, but we find in verse number nine that the Lord warns. I believe that it's necessary that God's people learn to appreciate the warnings that God gives in his word. The reality is that God's word is full of warnings. Warnings are given so that we can take heed to them, hearken unto them, so that we can avoid some adverse result or with regard to disobeying that warning or taking no concern for it, the consequences often are grave. The Bible says in verse number nine, as we think specifically of the warning that is given here, notice God says there shall no strange God be in thee, neither shalt thou worship any strange God. Uses the phrase strange God twice in verse number nine, and you know as well as I do, as we would study through the Old Testament, that idolatry was something that the children of Israel dealt with regularly. It was something that they constantly were seeming to go after strange gods and false gods. And we find that serving idols would bring judgment and God's delivering that warning, God's helping his people to understand that you should have no strange God within you, that there shall no strange God be in thee, neither shalt thou worship any strange God. That's unfortunate, we understand. and as we study through the Old Testament, that they regularly had no regard for this type of warning that God gave. In fact, as you think about the warnings, turn over to Psalm 19, if you will. And I want you to notice within the context of Psalm 19 and speaking about the law of the Lord and how wonderful God's word is, the Bible in that text talks about being warned. And again, warnings are given for our benefit. We find in verse number 11 of Psalm 19, notice the Bible says, "'Moreover, by them.'" By what? Well, by the law of the Lord, the statutes of the Lord, the commandments of the Lord. He says, "'Moreover, by them is thy servant warned, "'and in keeping of them there is great reward.'" This evening, as much as we find that God instructs, we also find that God warns. And God gives us warnings. He gives you warnings and His word is full of warnings. And it's important even as I preached on Sunday evening when the Lord asked the question, "'Have you not read?' It's important that we're daily, regularly in God's word. Why? So that we can receive the instruction that God has for us, but also so that we can receive the warnings that God has for us. I know that you have, no doubt in my mind, you have materials in your home. We have things here in our church that have labels on them that are called warning labels. And oftentimes there are warning labels that may be on cleaning chemicals and solutions and what have you. And there's certain instructions and warning with regard to inhaling in a closed in area and the adverse effects that could come upon you. Having irritant on your skin with certain chemicals that you work with and all of that ingesting. If you ever have a child that you try to make sure those cleaning supplies are locked up and unavailable. Why? Because you don't want that child to consume those particular items. Why? Because there's a danger. Somebody, the FDA requires that there are warning labels on things that are sold. Now, some of the warning labels are kind of like you scratch your head and say, really you order hot coffee at McDonald's and it says warning contents may be hot. And you say, well, I hope they are. If they're not, I want a refund. I want something that's hot. But you understand that long ago, somebody sued them because they got burnt with hot coffee and got a large settlement. And now they put that warning on there. But we find this evening that God has given to us warnings and it's so important, the children of Israel, they're here in this situation and God is providing them with all that they need. We find the Lord instructs, we find that the Lord warns. Then I want you to notice as we get into verse number 10, notice what the Bible says here in verse number 10. Number three, if you're taking notes, the Lord reminds. The Bible says this, I am the Lord thy God, which brought thee out of the land of Egypt. As we just ponder the first half of verse number 10, consider with me how the Lord reminds them of some very important truth. Number one, he reminds them of who he is. I am the Lord thy God. I hope this evening that you understand that as you've been saved by the grace of God through the precious blood of Christ that was shed on Calvary, that you understand that he is your father and you're his child and oh, what a great truth to know that he is mine and I am his. Isn't that wonderful? And we begin to think about the truth that God is saying to his people here, I am the Lord thy God, but then he also not only reminds them who he is, but he reminds them what he did for them, which brought thee out of the land of Egypt. Just reading those particular words bring back flood, even in my mind, not being a Hebrew, not being one that would have had real close ties with all of that, but man, reading the Bible and understanding and you read those words that brought thee out of the land of Egypt, my mind immediately runs back to the book of Exodus and immediately being reminded of the power of God that was displayed on their behalf to deliver them. Isn't that wonderful? And just that reminder that God would give them. And of course, we note, as we mentioned about the Feast of Tabernacles, that being part of the whole aspect of the particular feast and here he's reminding them that he brought them out of the land of Egypt. You know, if you go back to the book of Exodus just quickly this evening, look if you will, in Exodus chapter 32. And as you begin to understand kind of what's going on here and how God is dealing with his people, we find that, and we don't have time to deal with all of it, but I want you to notice here in Exodus chapter 32, notice what happens. Moses is up on the mountain here in Exodus chapter 32, we find that God announces to Moses in verse number seven, go get thee down for thy people, which thou broughtest out of the land of Egypt have corrupted themselves. Verse eight, they have turned aside quickly out of the way which I commanded them. They have made them a molten calf and have worshiped it and have sacrificed their own two and said, notice what they said, these be thy gods, O Israel. Then notice this, which have brought thee up out of the land of Egypt. We find that as Moses and Joshua come down and they hear, as it says in verse number 17, at the end of the verse, there is a noise of war in the camp that Joshua and Moses have this conversation in verse 18, and he said, it is not the voice of them that shout for mastery, victory, neither is it the voice of them that cry for being overcome, defeat, but the noise of them that sing do I hear. And it came to pass as soon as he came nigh into the camp that he saw the calf and the dancing and Moses's anger waxed hot and he cast the tables out of his hands and break them beneath the mountain. He took the calf, which they had made and burnt it in the fire and ground it to powder and strotted upon the water and made the children of Israel drink of it. I am the Lord thy God, which brought thee out of the land of Egypt. You know, it is a good thing when God's people are reminded of truth. Sometimes we need to be reminded of some things. Sometimes it's good for us to be reminded. And it was a routine, it was something that we did, no matter what level you were at, you started back at the basics. I know it's a precious truth, and just to be reminded that He's my God. And then when you're reminded what He's done for you. He did for you what you could not do for yourself. He provided for you what you could not provide, what no one else could provide for you. And it causes us, when we have that time, when God reminds us, it causes us to just pause and just to rejoice in who He is and what He has done for us. And here in verse number 10, I see exactly that is taking place, that God is reminding His people, here's who I am and here's what I've done for you. And based upon what He's done for them, we then find that He finishes verse number 10 with the understanding of who He is and what He has done. He then finishes up verse number 10 by saying this, Open thy mouth wide, and I will fill it. The fourth thought that I have this evening as we look at these few verses of Scripture is this, the Lord satisfies. The Lord instructs. I'm so thankful for God's instruction. The Lord warns. I'm thankful for the warnings. I know how taking heed to the warnings will keep us out of trouble. And sometimes we say, how did I get here? What happened? Well, you didn't take heed to the warning. You didn't follow the instruction. And we can rejoice in how God reminds us. But then as we come to the end of verse number 10, and we consider that God says open thy mouth wide, and I will fill it. You know, the Lord satisfies. There are a lot of people in our world today, Christians included, that are looking for satisfaction in all sorts of things. They think that the next promotion will provide them with some level of satisfaction. They think that this relationship or this measure of power or this extra money, whatever it is, something is going to satisfy. But can I tell you that nothing the world has will satisfy you like God will. Notice if you will, in Psalm 107, notice what the Bible says just a few pages over in Psalm 107. It's a truth that must be just something we meditate upon this evening, understanding that the Lord satisfies. He says, open thy mouth wide, and I will fill it. Further in Psalm 107 in verse number 9, for he satisfieth the longing soul, and filleth the hungry soul with goodness. He satisfies. You know, only Jesus satisfies. And we can try to look for satisfaction, as I mentioned a moment ago, in all kinds of places and all kinds of things. But it's all vain, because only Jesus satisfies. The things of this world, as the songwriter said, as we turn our eyes upon Jesus, the things of this world will grow strangely dim, see, because God satisfies. And here what God wanted His people to recognize as He's speaking to them and as this psalm would be used during the Feast of Tabernacles, He's reminding them that here's who He is, here's what He has done, and here's what He will continue to do. He will satisfy. Isn't it unfortunate that the children of Israel turned to all sorts of different places and things? We know what happens. But as we examine our own lives, we ask ourselves the question today, will we hearken? Will we have an ear to hear what God says with the purpose of obeying, to observe and obey, to give heed to the truth that God has given to us? I hope this evening that we would respond different than how the children of Israel did. There wouldn't be said of you or of me that we hearkened not. Let's pray. Father, we love you tonight. We thank you so much for loving us. I thank you for the way in which you instruct us. We're thankful that we have a Bible. It's a privilege to have your Word. It really is. It's not enough to have your Word. We must read it. We must study it. We must hear and obey. We must hearken. Thank you for the instruction that you provide. Thank you for the warnings. Oh, how we need to be warned. I pray that you'd help us to take heed to the warnings. Thank you for the reminders. God, you've been so good to us. We're thankful for who you are and for what you've done. Thank you that we know you personally as our Lord and Savior, our Father. I'm thankful that you satisfy. Lord, what a blessing to know that you can give us exactly what we need. You know what it is that we need better than we do. God, we're thankful that you satisfy. We thank you that you're a God who hears and answers and we pray that you'd be honored and glorified through the remainder of the service tonight. For it's in Christ's name we pray. Amen.
